title: Skills, Tasks and Technologies: Implications for Employment and Earnings

statistical_results: A college premium of 68 log points implies that earnings of the average college graduate in 2008 exceeded those of the average high school graduate by 97 percent (i.e., exp(0.68) − 1 ≈ 0.974).
the college premium stood at 68 points in 2008, a high water mark for the full sample period
the college premium in 2005 was at its highest level since 1915
Log wage growth in this ten year period averaged approximately 20 percent.

Real earnings of males with less than a four year college degree fell steeply between 1979 and 1992, by 12 log points for high school and some-college males, and by 20 log points for high school dropouts.
the slope of the 1973 locus then traces out the implied log earnings gain for each additional year of schooling in 1973, up to 18 years
Between 1973 and 1989, the slope steepened while the intercept fell by a sizable 10 log points.
at 18 years of schooling, it lies 16 log points above the 1989 locus
the male 90th percentile rose steeply and almost monotonically between 1979 and 2007
the male median was essentially flat from 1980 to 1994
the male 10th percentile fell steeply (paralleling the trajectory of high school dropout wages)
the female 90/50 rises nearly continuously from the late 1970s forward
the female 50/10 expands rapidly during the 1980s, plateaus through the mid-1990s, and then compresses modestly thereafter
a substantial fraction (13 percent) of all female hours worked in 1979 were paid at or below the federal minimum wage
the real value of which declined by 30 log points over the subsequent 9 years

In 1979, the four middle skill occupations–sales, office and administrative workers, production workers, and operatives–accounted for 57.3 percent of employment. In 2007, this number was 48.6 percent, and in 2009, it was 45.7 percent.
The correlation between occupational growth rates in 1979-1989 and 1989-1999 is 0.53, and for the decades of 1989-1999 and 1999-2009, it is 0.74.
the correlation between occupational growth rates during 1999-2007 and 2007-2009–that is, prior to and during the current recession–is 0.76

In all 16 countries depicted, middle-wage occupations decline as a share of employment. The largest declines occur in France and Austria (by 12 and 14 percentage points, respectively) and the smallest occurs in Portugal (1 percentage point). The unweighted average decline in middle-skill employment across countries is 8 percentage points.

In 13 of 16 countries, high-wage occupations increased their share of employment, with an average gain of 6 percentage points, while low-wage occupations grew as a share of employment in 11 of 16 countries.
In all 16 countries, low-wage occupations increased in size relative to middle-wage occupations, with a mean gain in employment in low relative to middle wage occupations of 10 percentage points.

Nordhaus (2007) estimates that the real cost of performing a standardized set of computational tasks–where cost is expressed in constant dollars or measured relative to the labor cost of performing the same calculations–fell by at least 1.7 trillion-fold between 1850 and 2006, with the bulk of this decline occurring in the last three decades.
Nordhaus estimates that between 1980 and 2006, the real cost of performing a standardized set of computations fell by 60 to 75 percent annually.
ln P = constant + 0.027 × t − 0.612 · ln(S_H/S_L) with standard errors (0.005) (0.128)

these estimates suggest that the evolution of the college premium during the period 1963 through 1987 can be characterized by an elasticity of substitution between college graduate workers and non-college workers of about σ̂ = 1.061 ≈ 1.6, and an annual increase of about 27 percent in the relative demand for college labor
When fit to this longer time period, the model yields a substantially higher estimate of the elasticity of substitution, σ̂ ≈ 2.9 and a lower trend rate of demand growth (16 percent annually).
While the observed college premium rose by 12 points between 1992 and 2008, the model predicts a rise of 25 log points.
the employment-weighted correlation of U.S. and E.U. changes in employment shares by occupation is 0.63

Between 1940 and 1980, the fraction of young adults ages 25 to 34 who had completed a four-year college degree at the start of each decade increased three-fold among both sexes, from 5 percent and 7 percent among females and males, respectively, in 1940 to 20 percent and 27 percent, respectively, in 1980.
This gap stood at positive 7 percentage points in 1980 and negative 7 percentage points in 2008.

the fraction of employed males who had high school or lower education fell from 57 to 42 percent between 1979 and 2007, while the fraction with at least a four-year college degree rose from 20 to 28 percent

the share of males employed in service occupations rose by 4.4 percentage points between 1979 and 2007 while the share in professional, technical and managerial occupations rose by 5.3 percentage points

For females, 85 percent of the decline in middle-skill jobs was offset by a rise in professional, managerial and technical occupations. For males, this share is 55 percent, with the remaining 45 percent accruing to service occupations.

After hovering at 41 to 43 percent of female employment during 1959 through 1979, the share of females employed in clerical, administrative support and sales occupations fell in each of the next three decades, with a net decline of 8 percentage points.

Between 1959 and 1989, the share of females employed in service occupations declined from 23.2 to 17.2 percent. It then rebounded. Between 1989 and 2007, female employment in service occupations rose by 4.2 percentage points (25 percent)
ambiguity_or_caveats: A potential concern with the interpretation of these results is that the measured real wage declines of less-educated workers mask an increase in their total compensation after accounting for the rising value of employer provided non-wage benefits such as healthcare, vacation and sick time.
Careful analysis of representative, wage and fringe benefits data by Pierce (2001 and forthcoming) casts doubt on this notion, however.
Monetizing the value of these benefits does not substantially alter the conclusion that real compensation for low-skilled workers fell in the 1980s.
the estimated falls in real wages would also be overstated if the price deflator overestimated the rate of inflation and thus underestimated real wage growth
The PCE generally shows a lower rate of inflation than the more commonly used Consumer Price Index (CPI)
It is unclear whether housing costs should be fully netted out of the consumer wage, however.

Whether the measured rise in inequality in the 1970s is reliable has been a subject of some debate because this increase is detected in the Census and CPS March series but not in the contemporaneous May CPS series
Recent evidence appears to support the veracity of the 1970s inequality increase.

The major redesign of the earnings questions in the CPS ORG in 1994 led to a substantial rise in non-response to these questions as well as other potential consistency issues that are only imperfectly addressed by our processing of the data.

the earnings non-response rate in the CPS ORG increased from 15.3 percent in 1993 to 23.3 percent in the last quarter of 1995 (the first quarter in which allocation flags are available in the redesigned survey), and reached 31 percent by 2001
This redesign may be an important factor in accounting for the significant discrepancies in trends in inequality trends in the May/ORG and March samples beginning in 1994
the CPS and Census samples are unlikely to provide accurate measures of earnings at the highest and lowest percentiles

High percentiles are unreliable both because high earnings values are truncated in public use samples and, more importantly, because non-response and under-reporting are particularly severe among high income households.

wage earnings in the lower percentiles imply levels of consumption that lie substantially below observed levels (Meyer and Sullivan, 2008). This disparity reflects a combination of measurement error, under-reporting, and transfer income among low wage individuals.
A potential explanation for the discrepancy is that the earnings data collected by the March CPS uses a broader earnings construct, and in particular is more likely to capture bonus and performance.
An alternative explanation for the March versus May/ORG discrepancy is deterioration in data quality.

the sharp rise in earnings non-response in the May/ORG CPS following the 1994 survey redesign may have reduced the consistency of the wage series (especially given the sharp rise in earnings non-response following the redesign)
we have found that it is infeasible to develop a satisfactory occupational classification scheme that is both detailed and consistent for the full 1959 through 2007 interval
the choice of weights–hours versus bodies–has only second-order effects on our substantive results

We stress that this initial cut of the data is intended as an example of how linking the comparative advantage of skill groups to changes over time in the demands for their task specialties could be used to explore and interpret the evolution of wages by skill group. The evidence in Table 10 is therefore only suggestive.

We stress that this interpretation of the gross patterns in the data is speculative and somewhat coarse. Our objective here is not to provide a definitive explanation for the rich set of facts offered by the data but rather to offer a set of tools that may be applied towards a more refined set of explanations.

We caution, however, that measures of both job tasks and offshorability are highly imperfect and differ substantively across studies. The conclusions drawn at this stage of the literature should therefore be viewed as provisional.
Firpo, Fortin and Lemieux (2009) find a significant role for offshorability in explaining wage polarization, though this effect is smaller than the estimated technology effect.
The efficacy of these measures relative to other task scales in predicting patterns of wage and employment polarization awaits testing.

notes: This is a comprehensive theoretical and empirical paper on labor market trends, not an experimental study. It presents a task-based framework for understanding how technology, skills, and tasks interact to shape employment and earnings distributions. The paper documents major labor market trends (1963-2008) including wage polarization, job polarization, and declining real wages for low-skill workers, then proposes a Ricardian task-based model as an alternative to the canonical two-skill model. The empirical analysis is largely descriptive and uses Census, CPS, and other administrative data sources rather than experimental designs. The paper includes extensive tables and figures documenting trends but does not report results from randomized experiments or quasi-experimental comparisons of treatment vs. control groups.
